summary: This guest post by Becky Jaffe celebrates the convergence of National Poem in Your Pocket Day, National Poetry Month, and Mathematics Awareness Month by curating a collection of math-themed poems. The selection ranges from Jacob Bernoulli's mystical meditation on infinity to playful limericks about pi and topology to Geoffrey Brock's narrative poem about switching from math to poetry. The centerpiece is Frederick Soddy's 'The Kiss Precise,' a proof of the Kissing Circles Theorem written entirely in verse and published in Nature in 1936, followed by two generalizations also written as poems. The post frames these works as a marriage of left and right brain hemispheres and challenges modern mathematicians to present their findings in verse.
key_points: Mathematical concepts from topology to calculus to number theory can be expressed through poetry, from limericks to formal verse
Frederick Soddy published an actual mathematical proof (the Kissing Circles Theorem) as a poem in Nature in 1936, and two mathematicians later extended it in verse
The collection spans centuries and tones — from Bernoulli's mystical reflections on infinity to comedic limericks about biblical approximations of pi
The post argues that math and poetry are complementary rather than opposed, celebrating their intersection during overlapping awareness months
money_quotes: Even as the finite encloses an infinite series And in the unlimited limits appear, So the soul of immensity dwells in minutia And in narrowest limits no limits inhere.
What joy to discern the minute in infinity! The vast to perceive in the small, what divinity!
The Topologist's child was quite hyper 'Til she wore a Moebius diaper. The mess on the inside Was thus on the outside And it was easy for someone to wipe her.
You disintegrate my differential, You dislocate my focus. My pulse goes up like an exponential whenever you cross my locus.
Four circles to the kissing come. The smaller are the benter. The bend is just the inverse of The distance form the center.
The sum of the squares of all four bends Is half the square of their sum.
In celebration of the marriage of the left and right hemispheres of the brain, I bring you a selection of poems dedicated to the fine art of mathematics – everything from the mystical to the sassy.
key_insight: Mathematics and poetry share a deep structural kinship — both seek elegance, compression, and surprise — and the tradition of expressing proofs in verse demonstrates that rigor and beauty are not opposing forces.
